techniques.title: System situational awareness
techniques.summary: Who am I, what privileges, what patch level, what is installed.
techniques.body: The first five minutes on a Windows foothold: identity and privileges (whoami /all), patch level (systeminfo), and local users/groups.

techniques.title: Run winPEAS
techniques.summary: Automated Windows privilege-escalation enumeration.

techniques.title: Service misconfiguration
techniques.summary: Unquoted service paths and weak service permissions to SYSTEM.
techniques.body: Two classic wins: an unquoted service path with a writable parent dir, and a service whose binary/config you can modify (sc config binPath=).

techniques.title: Token-privilege abuse (SeImpersonate)
techniques.summary: With SeImpersonate/SeAssignPrimaryToken, impersonate to SYSTEM.
techniques.body: Service accounts often hold SeImpersonatePrivilege. A "Potato"-family tool coerces a SYSTEM token. The exact variant/CLSID is OS-build-specific — match it to the target build; never assume a CLSID.

techniques.title: Credential harvesting
techniques.summary: Pull secrets from LSASS, SAM, and DPAPI once elevated.
techniques.body: With local admin/SYSTEM, dump LSASS (mimikatz/comsvcs), the local SAM, and DPAPI-protected secrets. Feed NT hashes back into the AD lateral cards.

techniques.title: Manual host enumeration
techniques.summary: Users, groups, services, scheduled tasks, and patch level by hand.
techniques.body: Complement winPEAS/Seatbelt/PrivescCheck with the manual set — local users and admins, services and their binary paths, scheduled tasks, and the hotfix list (feed systeminfo/wmic qfe into windows-exploit-suggester / wesng).

techniques.title: Stored credentials
techniques.summary: cmdkey-saved creds → runas without knowing the password.

techniques.title: Registry secrets
techniques.summary: Winlogon autologon password and saved PuTTY/VNC/SNMP creds.

techniques.title: Sensitive files
techniques.summary: unattend.xml / sysprep / web.config / PowerShell history.

techniques.title: DPAPI & browser creds (concept)
techniques.summary: Browser/credential-manager secrets are DPAPI-protected — decrypt with the masterkey.
techniques.body: DPAPI protects saved browser passwords, Wi-Fi keys and Credential Manager entries. With the user's masterkey (or SYSTEM) these decrypt — concept node; tooling (mimikatz dpapi / SharpDPAPI) is build-sensitive.

techniques.title: AlwaysInstallElevated
techniques.summary: If both policy keys are set, any MSI installs as SYSTEM.
techniques.body: When HKLM and HKCU both set AlwaysInstallElevated, an MSI you build runs as SYSTEM. Confirm both keys, then install a crafted package.

techniques.title: Privilege abuse (Se*)
techniques.summary: SeBackup/SeRestore/SeTakeOwnership/SeDebug/SeLoadDriver/SeManageVolume to SYSTEM.
techniques.body: Token privileges each enable a path: SeBackup/SeRestore read the SAM/SYSTEM hives, SeTakeOwnership claims protected objects, SeDebug touches any process, SeLoadDriver loads a vulnerable driver. Applicability depends on which privilege you hold — confirm with whoami /priv first.

techniques.title: DLL hijacking
techniques.summary: A privileged process loading a missing/writable DLL from a writable path.
techniques.body: Find a service/app that searches a writable directory for a DLL it can't find, and place a same-named DLL. Target- and search-order-specific — enumerate before planting.

techniques.title: Scheduled-task abuse
techniques.summary: A privileged scheduled task whose binary/script you can overwrite.

techniques.title: UAC bypass (methodology only)
techniques.summary: Auto-elevating binaries (e.g. fodhelper) can be coaxed — concept, no working bypass.
techniques.body: Some signed binaries auto-elevate and read attacker-controllable registry/paths (fodhelper, computerdefaults, sdclt). This is a responsible-use concept node: methodology only — Cephalo ships no working bypass string.

techniques.title: Kernel / driver LPE (version-gated)
techniques.summary: Match the patch level to a known LPE — e.g. PrintNightmare — and verify.
techniques.body: Feed systeminfo into windows-exploit-suggester / wesng to map missing patches to local privilege escalations such as PrintNightmare. All are build-specific — confirm the exact patch level against the cited advisory before any attempt.

techniques.title: Windows reverse shells
techniques.summary: PowerShell one-liner (have), nc.exe, or an msfvenom EXE.

techniques.title: Windows file transfer
techniques.summary: Invoke-WebRequest, bitsadmin, certutil (have), ftp, base64.

techniques.title: AMSI / LOLBAS reference
techniques.summary: Living-off-the-land binaries reference; AMSI/Defender is concept-only.
techniques.body: LOLBAS catalogs signed binaries that download/execute/proxy (certutil, bitsadmin, mshta, regsvr32, …). AMSI/Defender evasion is a responsible-use concept node — methodology only, no working bypass shipped.

techniques.title: Host discovery
techniques.summary: Identify live hosts on the subnet before per-host enumeration.
techniques.body: Start broad, then go deep. A ping sweep or ARP scan finds live hosts; follow with a full TCP port scan per host. Do not skip UDP on quiet boxes.

techniques.title: Nmap full enumeration
techniques.summary: Full TCP, then targeted service/version + default scripts; UDP top ports.
techniques.body: A two-stage pattern: a fast full-range TCP scan to discover open ports, then a focused service scan against just those ports. Run a UDP top-ports scan in parallel — SNMP/TFTP/DNS hide there.

techniques.title: SMB enumeration
techniques.summary: Enumerate shares, users, and policy over SMB/RPC.
techniques.body: SMB is a goldmine: null/guest sessions, readable shares, user enumeration, and password policy. On a domain-joined host this pivots straight into AD.

techniques.title: Web content discovery
techniques.summary: Fingerprint the stack and brute-force content/vhosts.
techniques.body: Identify the tech stack, then discover hidden content and virtual hosts. Feed findings back into targeted exploitation — never blind-fuzz forever.

techniques.title: FTP & SSH enumeration
techniques.summary: Banner-grab, anonymous FTP, and SSH auth methods.

techniques.title: Local privesc enumeration
techniques.summary: Automated + manual enumeration of the local attack surface.
techniques.body: Run an automated enumerator (linPEAS) but always corroborate manually: id, sudo -l, SUID set, writable cron, capabilities, and kernel version.

techniques.title: sudo rights abuse
techniques.summary: Abuse misconfigured sudo entries to run a root shell (GTFOBins).
techniques.body: sudo -l reveals allowed commands. If a binary is listed and has a GTFOBins sudo entry, it is almost certainly a root shell. Methodology only — confirm the exact binary against GTFOBins.

techniques.title: SUID/SGID binary abuse
techniques.summary: Find SUID-root binaries and abuse known ones (GTFOBins).

techniques.title: cron job abuse
techniques.summary: Hunt writable scripts run by root cron and tamper to escalate.

techniques.title: Kernel exploit (version-gated)
techniques.summary: Last resort — identify the kernel and search for a matching exploit.
techniques.body: Kernel exploits are version- and architecture-specific and can crash the box. Identify the exact kernel, then search a vetted source. Never run an offset/exploit you have not matched to the target version.

techniques.title: rustscan / masscan
techniques.summary: All-ports in seconds, then hand the open set to nmap for -sCV.

techniques.title: Bind shell
techniques.summary: Target listens; you connect in (useful when callbacks are filtered).

techniques.title: socat full-PTY shell
techniques.summary: A fully interactive TTY without the manual stty dance.

techniques.title: pwncat-cs
techniques.summary: A smart listener with auto-stabilisation and post-exploit modules.

techniques.title: Web shell (methodology)
techniques.summary: Where an upload/LFI allows, a minimal web shell gives command execution.
techniques.body: Methodology only: once you can place + reach a server-executable file, a tiny command-runner yields execution — then immediately upgrade to an interactive reverse shell via the VariableBar cards.

techniques.title: Manual deep enumeration
techniques.summary: Capabilities, listeners, sensitive-file perms, SSH keys, history.

techniques.title: pspy (process / cron spy)
techniques.summary: Watch processes and cron jobs fire without root — catches hidden scheduled tasks.

techniques.title: File transfer (base64 / nc / /dev/tcp)
techniques.summary: Move files when http/smb are blocked.

techniques.title: Loot collection
techniques.summary: Sweep for keys, password stores, configs and DB dumps.

techniques.title: Pivot with ligolo-ng / proxychains
techniques.summary: A tun-based pivot (ligolo-ng) or SOCKS via proxychains to reach the internal net.

techniques.title: Linux capabilities
techniques.summary: Find file capabilities (cap_setuid etc.) and abuse them via GTFOBins.

techniques.title: PATH hijacking
techniques.summary: A SUID/sudo binary calling another by relative name → drop a malicious one in $PATH.
techniques.body: If a privileged binary invokes a command without an absolute path, prepend a writable directory to $PATH and place a same-named script there.

techniques.title: Writable /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow
techniques.summary: Add a UID-0 user (passwd) or crack/replace a hash (shadow).

techniques.title: NFS no_root_squash
techniques.summary: On a no_root_squash export, drop a root-owned SUID binary from your box.
techniques.body: With root on your own host and an export mounted, create a root-owned SUID shell in the export; it executes as root on the target.

techniques.title: Docker / LXD group escape
techniques.summary: Membership in docker/lxd ≈ root — mount the host filesystem from a container.

techniques.title: LD_PRELOAD / LD_LIBRARY_PATH
techniques.summary: When sudo keeps LD_PRELOAD (env_keep), load a malicious shared object as root.

techniques.title: Writable service / systemd unit
techniques.summary: A writable unit file or service binary run by root → code execution as root.

techniques.title: Cron wildcard injection
techniques.summary: A root cron that globs `*` in a writable dir → smuggle option args (tar/rsync/chown).
techniques.body: When a root job runs e.g. tar cf backup.tar * in a directory you can write, drop files named like options (--checkpoint-action=...) so the wildcard expands into argument injection. The tar/GTFOBins entry shows the exact files.

techniques.title: Custom SUID binary analysis
techniques.summary: Reverse a non-standard SUID binary for relative calls, injectable args, or secrets.

techniques.title: Password reuse & creds in files
techniques.summary: Hunt configs/history/DBs for reusable passwords and keys.

techniques.title: pkexec PwnKit (version-gated)
techniques.summary: A well-known pkexec local privilege escalation — applies only to specific builds.
techniques.body: PwnKit is a memory-corruption local privilege escalation in pkexec. It is build-specific; confirm the target's pkexec version and patch level before relying on it. See the cited advisory for the exact affected range.

techniques.title: sudo Baron Samedit (version-gated)
techniques.summary: A heap overflow in sudo — applies only to specific sudo versions.
techniques.body: Baron Samedit is a sudo heap overflow reachable without sudo rights on affected versions. Check the sudo version against the advisory before assuming exploitability.

techniques.title: DNS (53)
techniques.summary: Banner, zone transfer (AXFR), and subdomain brute-forcing.

techniques.title: POP3 / IMAP (110/143)
techniques.summary: Banner-grab and authenticated mailbox access.

techniques.title: NFS (111/2049)
techniques.summary: List exports, mount them, and note no_root_squash for privesc.
techniques.body: showmount -e lists exports; mount them locally to read/write. An export with no_root_squash lets a local root drop a root-owned SUID binary — see the NFS privesc node.

techniques.title: SNMP (161)
techniques.summary: Brute community strings, then walk the MIB for processes/users/creds.

techniques.title: Databases (1433 / 3306 / 5432)
techniques.summary: Version, default creds, and authenticated client queries (MSSQL/MySQL/Postgres).

techniques.title: Redis (6379)
techniques.summary: Unauthenticated Redis — info, keyspace, and known config-write vectors.
techniques.body: An unauthenticated Redis answers INFO/CONFIG. Where writable, config-set abuse (web-root or SSH-key write) is a known foothold vector — methodology only; confirm the target's filesystem layout first.

techniques.title: RDP / VNC (3389 / 5900)
techniques.summary: Connect with xfreerdp / vncviewer; BlueKeep is version-gated.

techniques.title: Elasticsearch / MongoDB (9200 / 27017)
techniques.summary: Unauthenticated NoSQL — dump indices/collections.

techniques.title: Legacy services (23/79/873)
techniques.summary: Telnet banner, finger user enum, rsync module listing.

techniques.title: LFI / RFI
techniques.summary: Read files / include remote code via a path parameter.
techniques.body: Test a path/include parameter for traversal. On PHP, php://filter base64-reads source, data:///expect:// may execute, and log poisoning turns LFI into RCE where a log path is includable. Methodology — confirm the inclusion point first.

techniques.title: File-upload bypass
techniques.summary: Smuggle an executable upload past extension/content-type/magic checks.
techniques.body: Probe how the filter works (extension allow/deny, Content-Type, magic bytes, double extensions, null/case tricks) and where uploads land. Methodology only — the goal is to identify the bypass class, not ship a webshell.

techniques.title: Command injection
techniques.summary: Inject OS commands through an unsanitised parameter.
techniques.body: Test injection metacharacters (; | && $( ) backticks) with a benign, observable command first (timing or DNS callback), then escalate to a shell via the VariableBar reverse-shell cards.

techniques.title: SQL injection
techniques.summary: Union / boolean / time-based SQLi; automate with sqlmap.
techniques.body: Identify the injection (error/boolean/time), determine columns and DBMS, then extract. sqlmap automates detection and dumping; --os-shell / file-read are DBMS-and-privilege-gated. Methodology — confirm the parameter is injectable first.

techniques.title: Server-Side Template Injection
techniques.summary: Detect template evaluation, identify the engine, then read/exec per engine.
techniques.body: Submit a simple arithmetic expression in template syntax; if it evaluates, map the engine (Jinja2/Twig/Freemarker/…) and use the engine-specific read/exec primitive. Methodology — fingerprint before any payload.

techniques.title: XML External Entity (XXE)
techniques.summary: Abuse an XML parser that resolves external entities to read files / SSRF.
techniques.body: Where XML is parsed with external-entity resolution enabled, a declared entity can read local files or reach internal services (SSRF). Out-of-band variants exfiltrate via an attacker-hosted DTD. Methodology — confirm entity resolution first.

techniques.title: Insecure deserialization (concept)
techniques.summary: Untrusted serialized objects → code execution; identify the format first.
techniques.body: Recognise serialized blobs (PHP O:, Java rO0, .NET, Python pickle) and the sink that deserializes them. Exploitation is gadget-chain dependent — concept node; identify the format and entry point, then research the specific chain.

techniques.title: Auth bypass / IDOR / default creds
techniques.summary: Logic flaws, object-reference tampering, and forgotten default credentials.
techniques.body: Try default/weak credentials, tamper object identifiers for IDOR, and probe auth logic (forced browsing, parameter pollution, JWT/none). Feed any recovered creds into the VariableBar to pivot to a shell.

techniques.title: Catch a shell
techniques.summary: Stand up a listener (and make it comfortable).

techniques.title: Reverse shell one-liners
techniques.summary: Canonical bash / nc / PowerShell callbacks (RevShells loop).
techniques.body: The original RevShells use case: set LHOST/LPORT once and copy the right callback for the target. Methodology only — these are canonical syntaxes, not an exploit.

techniques.title: Stabilise a TTY
techniques.summary: Upgrade a dumb shell to a fully interactive PTY.

techniques.title: Serve files from your box
techniques.summary: HTTP / SMB servers to deliver tooling.

techniques.title: Pull files onto the target
techniques.summary: Download tooling on Linux (wget/curl) and Windows (certutil/iwr).

techniques.title: Pivot with chisel
techniques.summary: Reverse SOCKS proxy through a foothold to reach the internal net.
techniques.body: Run a chisel server on your box and a reverse client on the foothold to expose a SOCKS proxy; point proxychains at it for deep enumeration.

techniques.title: SSH dynamic port-forward
techniques.summary: A SOCKS proxy over SSH when you hold creds on a Linux pivot.

techniques.title: WriteOwner → take ownership
techniques.summary: Set yourself as owner of the target object, then grant yourself rights.
techniques.body: A WriteOwner edge lets you make yourself the object's owner; the owner can always write the DACL, so chain WriteOwner → WriteDACL → full control.

techniques.title: WriteDACL → grant rights
techniques.summary: Write a new ACE granting yourself control (e.g. DCSync on the domain).
techniques.body: With WriteDACL over the domain object you can grant yourself the replication rights needed for DCSync; over a user, grant GenericAll to reset/roast them.

techniques.title: GPO abuse (methodology)
techniques.summary: Edit a GPO you control to run code on linked computers/users.
techniques.body: With write rights over a GPO (or its link), you can push an immediate scheduled task or startup script to every object in scope. Exact tooling and link scope are environment-specific — confirm the GPO's links before acting.

techniques.title: AdminSDHolder persistence
techniques.summary: Write a DACL on AdminSDHolder; SDProp re-applies it to protected groups.
techniques.body: The AdminSDHolder object's DACL is stamped onto protected groups (Domain Admins etc.) by SDProp on a timer. Granting yourself rights there is durable domain persistence — methodology only.

techniques.title: GenericWrite/AddMember → group control
techniques.summary: Add a controlled principal to a privileged group via a writable ACL.
techniques.body: A GenericAll/GenericWrite/AddMember edge over a group lets you add yourself (or a controlled user) to it — instantly inheriting its rights.

techniques.title: ForceChangePassword → reset
techniques.summary: Reset a target user's password without knowing the old one.

techniques.title: AddKeyCredentialLink → Shadow Credentials
techniques.summary: Write a key credential, then PKINIT for the target's NT hash/TGT.
techniques.body: With write access to msDS-KeyCredentialLink, add a device key and PKINIT to recover the target's hash. ADCS/PKINIT prerequisites are environment-specific.

techniques.title: Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD)
techniques.summary: Write RBCD to a controlled computer, then S4U to impersonate any user.
techniques.body: Writing msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity on a target computer to a controlled machine account lets you S4U2Self/Proxy and impersonate any user (e.g. Administrator) to that host's services.

techniques.title: ESC1 — enrollee-supplied SAN
techniques.summary: A template lets the enrollee specify the SAN — request a cert AS a DA.
techniques.body: ESC1: the template has "Enrollee Supplies Subject" + client-auth EKU and is enrollable by low-priv users. Request a certificate with an -upn of a privileged account, then authenticate as them. Prerequisites are environment-specific — confirm with certipy find -vulnerable first.

techniques.title: ESC2 / ESC3 / ESC4 — Any-Purpose, Enrollment Agent, template ACL
techniques.summary: Any-Purpose/SubCA EKUs (ESC2), Enrollment-Agent abuse (ESC3), writable template (ESC4).
techniques.body: ESC2 (Any Purpose / no EKU), ESC3 (Certificate Request Agent EKU → enroll on behalf of others), ESC4 (you hold write rights over the template, so make it ESC1-vulnerable, then exploit). All are prerequisite-specific — verify before use.

techniques.title: ESC6 / ESC7 — CA flag & CA roles
techniques.summary: EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 on the CA (ESC6); CA Officer/Manager rights (ESC7).
techniques.body: ESC6: the CA honours requester-supplied SANs globally (an attribute flag set on the CA) — any template becomes ESC1-like. ESC7: with ManageCA / ManageCertificates you can add yourself as an officer and issue/approve requests. Confirm the CA state first.

techniques.title: ESC8 — NTLM relay to web enrollment
techniques.summary: Relay a coerced machine's NTLM auth to the ADCS web-enrollment endpoint.
techniques.body: ESC8 weaponises the HTTP enrollment interface: coerce a target (often a DC) and relay its NTLM auth to /certsrv to mint a certificate as that machine — see the NTLM-relay technique for the runnable ntlmrelayx command.

techniques.title: Certificate → TGT (PKINIT / UnPAC-the-hash)
techniques.summary: Authenticate with a minted certificate to recover a TGT and the NT hash.
techniques.body: Once you hold a client-auth certificate for a principal, PKINIT yields a TGT; certipy's auth (or PKINITtools) also recovers the account's NT hash via UnPAC-the-hash. The certificate flow itself is canonical and not template-specific.

techniques.title: ESC9 / ESC10 / ESC11 (noted)
techniques.summary: szOID / no-security-extension and RPC-enrollment variants — verify per environment.
techniques.body: ESC9 (no security extension), ESC10 (weak certificate mappings), ESC11 (RPC enrollment relay) are newer and highly environment-specific. Treat as leads to validate with certipy in the target before relying on them.

techniques.title: NTLM authentication flow
techniques.summary: Challenge/response over the NT hash — and why the hash itself is enough.
techniques.body: NTLM is a challenge/response handshake: the server sends a challenge, the client returns a response computed from the account's NT hash. Because the response derives from the hash, not the password, possessing the NT hash is sufficient to authenticate — this is what makes Pass-the-Hash work. Captured NetNTLMv2 responses are crackable offline or relayable live (signing permitting).

techniques.title: Kerberos flow (AS-REQ → TGT → TGS)
techniques.summary: TGT then per-service TGS — and where each ticket can be abused.
techniques.body: The client sends an AS-REQ (pre-auth encrypted with its key) and receives a TGT encrypted with the krbtgt key. It then presents the TGT in a TGS-REQ to obtain a service ticket (TGS) for an SPN. Abuses map cleanly onto the flow: AS-REP roasting (no pre-auth), Kerberoasting (the TGS for an SPN), overpass-the- hash (hash/key → TGT), Golden (forge a TGT from krbtgt), Silver (forge a TGS from a service key).

techniques.title: Credential forms map
techniques.summary: What each credential form (cleartext / NT hash / AES / TGT / TGS / DPAPI) unlocks.
techniques.body: Cleartext → anything (auth, Kerberos, NTLM). NT hash → Pass-the-Hash + overpass- the-hash (→TGT). AES key → Pass-the-Key (quieter than RC4). TGT → Pass-the-Ticket to any service the user can reach. TGS → one specific service (Silver/Kerberoast). DPAPI masterkeys → decrypt browser/credential-manager secrets. Knowing which form you hold tells you exactly which lateral cards apply.

techniques.title: Path composition → Domain Admin
techniques.summary: How roasting, ACLs, delegation and ADCS chain into a path to DA.
techniques.body: Real domains fall by composition, not a single bug: a sprayed/roasted credential gives a foothold; a BloodHound ACL edge (GenericAll/WriteDACL) grants control of a better principal; delegation or ADCS (ESC) turns that into a privileged ticket/cert; DCSync or NTDS extraction finishes it. The AD mindmap's "Have valid creds?" decision and its converging edges to Domain Admin are exactly these composed paths — follow the glowing lineage.

techniques.title: SMB enumeration (AD lens)
techniques.summary: Enumerate domain hosts, shares, users and password policy over SMB.
techniques.body: In a domain, SMB enumeration doubles as AD recon. Run it across the standard toolset — each surfaces a slightly different view: netexec (users/policy/shares in one sweep + signing status), smbmap (per-share READ/WRITE), rpcclient (SAMR/LSARPC user+group dump), smbclient (raw share access). A null session (-u '' -p '') often works pre-creds; watch the banner for signing:False (relay target) and READ/WRITE share perms.

techniques.title: LDAP enumeration
techniques.summary: Pull users, groups and computer objects over LDAP.

techniques.title: Kerberos user enumeration
techniques.summary: Validate usernames over Kerberos pre-auth without lockout.
techniques.body: Kerbrute's userenum distinguishes valid from invalid principals via the KDC's pre-auth response — quietly, without touching the lockout counter.

techniques.title: BloodHound collection
techniques.summary: Collect the domain graph for attack-path analysis.
techniques.body: Run a remote collector (bloodhound.py) or on-host SharpHound, then import the zip into BloodHound CE and run the cypher library (see the BloodHound section).

techniques.title: AS-REP Roasting
techniques.summary: Recover crackable AS-REP hashes for users without Kerberos pre-auth.
techniques.body: Accounts flagged DONT_REQ_PREAUTH return a crackable AS-REP. The Impacket route works without credentials when fed a user list; netexec and Rubeus do it authenticated. Crack the $krb5asrep$ hash with hashcat mode 18200 (john: krb5asrep). OPSEC: requesting many AS-REPs is loud in event 4768.

techniques.title: Kerberoasting
techniques.summary: Request TGS-REP tickets for SPN accounts and crack them offline.
techniques.body: Any authenticated user can request service tickets for SPN-bearing accounts; the encrypted portion is crackable offline (hashcat 13100). Tool-flavour variants below — Impacket, netexec or Rubeus.

techniques.title: DCSync
techniques.summary: Replicate password hashes from the DC with replication rights.
techniques.body: A principal with DS-Replication-Get-Changes(-All) can ask the DC to replicate secrets — including krbtgt. secretsdump performs the DRSUAPI DCSync; the card swaps auth between password, NT hash and Kerberos.

techniques.title: ADCS abuse (ESC, prereq-gated)
techniques.summary: Find vulnerable certificate templates; abuse is template-prereq-specific.
techniques.body: ADCS ESC1-ESC16 abuses depend on exact template/CA prerequisites that vary per environment — enumerate first, then match the specific ESC. Never assume a template is vulnerable without confirming its prerequisites.

techniques.title: PsExec lateral movement
techniques.summary: SYSTEM shell over SMB via a service — password, PtH, or PtT.
techniques.body: Impacket psexec drops a service and returns a SYSTEM shell. Noisy (writes a binary, creates a service). Choose the auth mode with the credMode switch.

techniques.title: WMIExec lateral movement
techniques.summary: Semi-interactive shell over WMI — quieter than psexec.

techniques.title: Evil-WinRM session
techniques.summary: Interactive PowerShell over WinRM — password, hash, or ticket.

techniques.title: Golden Ticket (methodology)
techniques.summary: Forge a TGT from the krbtgt key — domain persistence.
techniques.body: With the krbtgt NT hash and the domain SID you can forge arbitrary TGTs. This is total domain persistence — methodology + canonical syntax only.

techniques.title: Password spraying (lockout-aware)
techniques.summary: Try one password against many users — read the lockout policy FIRST.
techniques.body: Spray a single likely password across the user list. Always read the lockout threshold/observation window first and stay one attempt under it; prefer --continue-on-success to collect every hit in one pass.

techniques.title: LLMNR / NBT-NS / mDNS poisoning
techniques.summary: Answer broadcast name resolution to capture NetNTLMv2 hashes, then crack.
techniques.body: Responder poisons LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS and captures NetNTLMv2 challenge-responses from hosts looking for a name. Crack with hashcat -m 5600, or relay them live (see NTLM relay).

techniques.title: NTLM relay (ntlmrelayx)
techniques.summary: Relay captured NTLM auth to SMB / LDAP(S) / ADCS web-enroll (ESC8).
techniques.body: Instead of cracking a captured NetNTLMv2, relay it live to a service that does not require signing. SMB→LDAPS can grant RBCD or a DCSync ACL; SMB→ADCS web-enroll (ESC8) yields a certificate for the victim. Disable Responder's SMB/HTTP servers when relaying.

techniques.title: Authentication coercion (methodology)
techniques.summary: Force a machine account to authenticate to you to feed a relay.
techniques.body: Coercion methods (PrinterBug / PetitPotam / DFSCoerce / Coercer) trigger a target — often a DC — to authenticate to an attacker host, feeding an NTLM relay (e.g. to ADCS for ESC8). Exact applicability is patch- and version-dependent; confirm against the target before relying on any single method.

techniques.title: Hash & ticket cracking reference
techniques.summary: hashcat modes for the AD hash types you will collect.
techniques.body: NetNTLMv2 = 5600 · Kerberoast TGS-REP = 13100 · AS-REP = 18200 · NetNTLMv1 = 5500 · DCC2 (cached) = 2100. Match the mode to the capture.

techniques.title: Unconstrained delegation
techniques.summary: A host trusted for unconstrained delegation caches the TGT of any caller.
techniques.body: Compromise a host with unconstrained delegation, then coerce a privileged account (often a DC machine account) to authenticate to it — its full TGT is cached and can be extracted, then replayed. The coercion step is patch/version dependent (see authentication coercion).

techniques.title: Constrained delegation (S4U)
techniques.summary: Abuse S4U2self/S4U2proxy to impersonate any user to an allowed service.
techniques.body: An account configured for constrained delegation (msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo) can request a service ticket as an arbitrary user via S4U. Impacket getST forges the impersonated TGS for the allowed SPN.

techniques.title: Silver Ticket (service account)
techniques.summary: Forge a service ticket (TGS) from a service account's key — no DC contact.
techniques.body: With a service account's NT hash (or AES key) and the domain SID you can forge a TGS for that one service offline. Quieter than a Golden Ticket because the DC is never contacted — but scoped to the single SPN/service.

techniques.title: NTDS.dit extraction
techniques.summary: Pull every domain hash from NTDS.dit — over DRSUAPI, IFM, or a VSS copy.
techniques.body: The directory database (NTDS.dit) holds every account's hash. Extract it remotely via DRSUAPI (-just-dc), or on a DC via an ntdsutil IFM snapshot or a Volume Shadow Copy, then parse offline.

techniques.title: Diamond / Sapphire ticket
techniques.summary: Modify a real TGT in place (stealthier than a forged Golden Ticket).
techniques.body: A Diamond ticket decrypts a legitimately-issued TGT with the krbtgt key and re-stamps its PAC, so it survives PAC-validation better than a fully forged ticket. Highly tooling/version-dependent — validate in the target.

techniques.title: DC persistence (Skeleton / DSRM / SSP)
techniques.summary: Methodology-only domain persistence once you fully own the DC.
techniques.body: Skeleton Key (patch LSASS to accept a master password), DSRM logon abuse, and a custom Security Support Provider are all DC-resident persistence. EDR- and build-sensitive — methodology + concept only, never a turnkey implant.

techniques.title: Golden gMSA (concept)
techniques.summary: Recover gMSA key material to compute managed passwords offline (concept).
techniques.body: With the KDS root key material an attacker can compute any gMSA's managed password offline and indefinitely — durable persistence. Concept node; applicability and tooling are environment-specific.

techniques.title: LDAP anonymous bind enumeration
techniques.summary: Read naming contexts, users and computers over an anonymous LDAP bind.
techniques.body: Some DCs permit anonymous LDAP. Read the base DSE for naming contexts, then dump user/computer objects without credentials.

techniques.title: RID cycling / SID brute
techniques.summary: Enumerate domain accounts by brute-forcing RIDs off the domain SID.

techniques.title: AD DNS enumeration
techniques.summary: Discover DCs and services via SRV records; attempt a zone transfer.

techniques.title: SMB signing check
techniques.summary: Identify hosts with SMB signing not required — the relay targets.
techniques.body: NTLM relay only works against hosts where SMB signing is not required. The netexec banner reports signing status per host.

techniques.title: PowerView domain enumeration
techniques.summary: On-host AD recon (users, groups, ACLs, delegation) with PowerView.

techniques.title: ldapdomaindump
techniques.summary: Dump the whole directory (users/groups/computers/policy) to HTML/JSON.

techniques.title: GPP cpassword / SYSVOL
techniques.summary: Hunt SYSVOL for Group Policy Preferences passwords (decryptable cpassword).
techniques.body: GPP stored credentials in SYSVOL with a Microsoft-published AES key, so any authenticated user can recover them. netexec's gpp_password module decrypts.

techniques.title: LAPS & gMSA password read
techniques.summary: Read LAPS local-admin passwords and gMSA managed passwords you have rights to.

techniques.title: Domain trust enumeration
techniques.summary: Map trust relationships, direction and transitivity for cross-domain paths.

techniques.title: neo4j + BloodHound setup (legacy vs CE)
techniques.summary: Stand up neo4j + BloodHound and import collected data; mind the schema divergence.
techniques.body: Run neo4j, open BloodHound, and drag in the SharpHound/bloodhound-python zip. BloodHound CE and the legacy GUI differ in schema, node/edge names and some cypher field names — queries written for one may not run unchanged on the other. Treat cross-version cypher as [UNVERIFIED] until confirmed in your build.

techniques.title: enum4linux-ng
techniques.summary: One-shot SMB/RPC/LDAP enumeration (users, shares, policy, groups).

techniques.title: SMTP user-leak (feeds user lists)
techniques.summary: Enumerate valid users via SMTP VRFY/EXPN/RCPT to seed spraying/roasting.
techniques.body: A talkative SMTP server confirms which usernames exist (VRFY/EXPN/RCPT TO), seeding the user list you spray or roast. Cross-cuts Linux port 25.

techniques.title: Shadow Credentials (pyWhisker)
techniques.summary: Alternative AddKeyCredentialLink abuse via pyWhisker (→ PKINIT).
techniques.body: pyWhisker is the cross-platform alternative to certipy for writing msDS-KeyCredentialLink; pair it with PKINIT to recover the target's NT hash.

techniques.title: ADRecon
techniques.summary: Generate an offline report of the whole domain (users/groups/GPO/ACL/trusts).

techniques.title: Overpass-the-Hash / Pass-the-Key
techniques.summary: Turn an NT hash or AES key into a Kerberos TGT, then use the ticket.
techniques.body: "Overpass-the-hash" (a.k.a. pass-the-key) requests a real TGT from the KDC using only a credential's NT hash or AES key, sidestepping NTLM. Request the TGT, point KRB5CCNAME at the .ccache, and authenticate with -k -no-pass.

techniques.title: Pass-the-Ticket
techniques.summary: Inject a captured/forged ticket (.kirbi/.ccache) into your session.
techniques.body: Convert a Windows .kirbi to an Impacket .ccache (or vice-versa), export KRB5CCNAME, and every -k -no-pass tool wields the ticket.

techniques.title: Remote execution methods (Impacket spread)
techniques.summary: smbexec / atexec / dcomexec / winrs / RDP-PtH beyond psexec & wmiexec.
techniques.body: Each tool trades stealth for reliability. All Impacket exec tools accept the same auth forms as psexec (-hashes, -k), so PtH/PtT apply throughout.

techniques.title: Dump SAM / LSA / cached secrets
techniques.summary: secretsdump remotely or offline from saved hives.
techniques.body: With local-admin access, secretsdump pulls the local SAM, LSA secrets and domain cached creds (DCC2) in one shot — remotely over the wire, or offline from reg save hives. Feed the NT hashes back into the PtH lateral cards.

techniques.title: LSASS & DPAPI credential theft
techniques.summary: Parse LSASS remotely (lsassy) and loot DPAPI-protected secrets.
techniques.body: lsassy dumps + parses LSASS over the wire (methodology — on-host dumping is version/EDR-sensitive). DPAPI protects browser/credential-manager secrets; netexec's --dpapi recovers masterkeys + blobs you have rights to.

techniques.title: SID history abuse (child → parent)
techniques.summary: Within a forest, escalate from child-domain DA to the forest root via SID history.
techniques.body: A forged ticket carrying the Enterprise Admins SID in its SID-history field is honoured across an intra-forest trust (SID filtering is off inside a forest). With the child krbtgt key, forge a Golden Ticket that adds the parent's privileged SID, or let raiseChild automate the whole child→parent path.

techniques.title: Inter-forest trust key abuse
techniques.summary: Forge an inter-realm TGT from a captured trust key (SID filtering applies).
techniques.body: Across a forest trust, the shared trust key signs inter-realm referrals. Abuse is constrained by SID filtering and is highly environment-specific — enumerate the trust, then validate the path before relying on it.

bloodhound.title: Shortest path from Owned to Domain Admins
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH p=shortestPath((s)-[*1..]->(g:Group)) WHERE s.owned=true AND g.name='DOMAIN ADMINS@{{DOMAIN|upper}}' RETURN p
bloodhound.description: The headline query — any path from a node you own to Domain Admins.
bloodhound.abuse: Walk each edge on the returned path; every edge type maps to an abuse in the table below.

bloodhound.title: Kerberoastable users
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (u:User {hasspn:true}) RETURN u.name, u.serviceprincipalnames ORDER BY u.name
bloodhound.description: Users with a Service Principal Name — their TGS is crackable offline.
bloodhound.abuse: Request the TGS and crack it.

bloodhound.title: AS-REP roastable users
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (u:User {dontreqpreauth:true}) RETURN u.name ORDER BY u.name
bloodhound.description: Users without Kerberos pre-auth — their AS-REP is crackable offline.
bloodhound.abuse: Request the AS-REP and crack it.

bloodhound.title: Unconstrained delegation hosts
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (c:Computer {unconstraineddelegation:true}) RETURN c.name ORDER BY c.name
bloodhound.description: Computers trusted for unconstrained delegation cache TGTs of any caller.
bloodhound.abuse: Coerce a privileged auth to the host, then extract the cached TGT.

bloodhound.title: DCSync-capable principals
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (n)-[:GetChanges|GetChangesAll|DCSync]->(d:Domain {name:'{{DOMAIN|upper}}'}) RETURN n.name
bloodhound.description: Principals holding replication rights — i.e. anyone who can DCSync.
bloodhound.abuse: Replicate secrets (incl. krbtgt) from the DC.

bloodhound.title: AdminTo from owned principals
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH p=(s)-[:AdminTo|MemberOf*1..]->(c:Computer) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN p
bloodhound.description: Hosts where a principal you own has local admin.
bloodhound.abuse: Authenticate to the host as admin and execute.

bloodhound.title: Dangerous outbound ACLs from owned
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH p=(s)-[:GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner|Owns|ForceChangePassword|AddMember*1..]->(t) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN p
bloodhound.description: Outbound control edges you can abuse from nodes you already own.
bloodhound.abuse: Walk each edge using the edge→abuse table.

bloodhound.title: Sessions on high-value targets
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (u:User)-[:HasSession]->(c:Computer) WHERE u.highvalue=true RETURN u.name, c.name
bloodhound.description: Where high-value users have live sessions (credential-theft targets).
bloodhound.abuse: Compromise the host and steal the session's credentials/tickets.

bloodhound.title: RBCD-writable targets
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH p=(s)-[:GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|Owns]->(c:Computer) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN p
bloodhound.description: Computers whose msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity you can write.
bloodhound.abuse: Configure RBCD to a controlled account, then S4U to impersonate.

bloodhound.title: Mark a principal as owned
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (n) WHERE n.name='{{USER}}@{{DOMAIN|upper}}' SET n.owned=true RETURN n.name
bloodhound.description: Tag a compromised principal so path queries start from it.
bloodhound.abuse: Run after each compromise to keep Owned→DA paths current.

bloodhound.title: Edge: DCSync / GetChanges / GetChangesAll
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (n)-[:GetChanges|GetChangesAll]->(d:Domain) RETURN n.name
bloodhound.description: Grants the right to replicate directory secrets from the domain.
bloodhound.abuse: Run a DCSync (secretsdump) to pull NT hashes including krbtgt.

bloodhound.title: Edge: AdminTo
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:AdminTo]->(c:Computer) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, c.name
bloodhound.description: Local administrator on the target computer.
bloodhound.abuse: Authenticate as admin and get a SYSTEM shell.

bloodhound.title: Edge: CanPSRemote
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:CanPSRemote]->(c:Computer) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, c.name
bloodhound.description: Member of Remote Management Users — WinRM access.
bloodhound.abuse: Open an interactive PowerShell session over WinRM.

bloodhound.title: Edge: GenericAll
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:GenericAll]->(t) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, labels(t), t.name
bloodhound.description: Full control over the target object.
bloodhound.abuse: Reset a user's password, add to a group, or write Shadow Credentials.

bloodhound.title: Edge: ForceChangePassword
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:ForceChangePassword]->(u:User) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, u.name
bloodhound.description: Reset the target user's password without knowing the old one.
bloodhound.abuse: Reset the password, then authenticate as the user.

bloodhound.title: Edge: AddKeyCredentialLink (Shadow Credentials)
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:AddKeyCredentialLink]->(t) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, t.name
bloodhound.description: Write to msDS-KeyCredentialLink — Shadow Credentials.
bloodhound.abuse: Add a key credential, then PKINIT for the target's NT hash/TGT.

bloodhound.title: Edge: AllowedToAct (RBCD)
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:AllowedToAct]->(c:Computer) RETURN s.name, c.name
bloodhound.description: Resource-Based Constrained Delegation configured to the principal.
bloodhound.abuse: S4U2Self/Proxy to impersonate any user to the target's services.

bloodhound.title: Edge: ReadGMSAPassword
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:ReadGMSAPassword]->(c:Computer) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, c.name
bloodhound.description: Read a gMSA managed password blob.
bloodhound.abuse: Recover the gMSA NT hash and use it for Pass-the-Hash.

bloodhound.title: Edge: ReadLAPSPassword
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:ReadLAPSPassword]->(c:Computer) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, c.name
bloodhound.description: Read the LAPS-managed local admin password of the computer.
bloodhound.abuse: Recover the local admin password and log in.

bloodhound.title: Edge: WriteOwner
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:WriteOwner]->(t) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, labels(t), t.name
bloodhound.description: Set yourself as the object's owner (owner can always write the DACL).
bloodhound.abuse: Take ownership, then WriteDACL to grant full control.

bloodhound.title: Edge: WriteDacl
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:WriteDacl]->(t) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, labels(t), t.name
bloodhound.description: Write a new ACE on the target — incl. DCSync rights on the domain.
bloodhound.abuse: Grant yourself FullControl or replication (DCSync) rights.

bloodhound.title: Edge: GPLink / writable GPO
bloodhound.cypher: MATCH (s)-[:GenericWrite|WriteDacl|Owns]->(g:GPO) WHERE s.owned=true RETURN s.name, g.name
bloodhound.description: Control over a GPO (or its link) linked to users/computers.
bloodhound.abuse: Push an immediate scheduled task/script to objects in the GPO's scope.
